query: clinical trials and efficacy of ozanimod in ulcerative colitis

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib's indication as a treatment for ulcerative colitis (UC) was approved by the FDA following three randomized phase III placebo-controlled clinical trials (OCTAVE Sustain trial, OCTAVE Induction 1, OCTAVE Induction 2), which were conducted following a promising phase 2 trial. [4] The agent is the first medication in the JAK inhibitors class of drugs to be approved in the UC management. [5] Similar to RA and PA, tofacitinib's indication in UC is for adult patients with active moderate or severe disease who have not demonstrated an adequate response to TNF blockers or intolerance to therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Ulcerative Colitis
retrieved_docs.content: The initial dose of golimumab is 200 mg (subcutaneous). At week 2, 100 mg (subcutaneous), followed by 100 mg every 4 weeks

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Administration -- Ulcerative Colitis
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended initial dose of upadacitinib for induction therapy is 45 mg once daily for 8 weeks (induction phase). For maintenance therapy, the recommended dosage is 15 mg once daily. In cases of severe, refractory, or extensive ulcerative colitis, a dosage of 30 mg once daily may be considered. If an adequate therapeutic response is not attained with the 30 mg dose, upadacitinib should be discontinued. Using the lowest effective dosage required to sustain the desired response is advised.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids are primarily used to induce remission in moderate-to-severe Crohn disease and should be used sparingly to avoid side effects. Immunomodulators serve as adjuncts that reduce immunogenicity against biologics. Notably, combination therapy with an immunomodulator and anti-TNF is more effective than monotherapy with either medication. [33] Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab, and certolizumab pegol) block the downstream effects of the TNF inflammatory cascade. These agents are efficacious in steroid-resistant or immunomodulator-refractory Crohn disease. The effect onset is relatively rapid, with clinical benefit seen within 2 weeks of initiation. Anti-TNF drugs are effective in treating fistulizing disease and reducing the risk of postsurgical endoscopic recurrence. [34] However, caution is advised when using these agents in patients with a history of demyelinating disease, congestive heart failure, and malignancies like lymphoma. [35] Recent data demonstrate no associated increase in adverse maternal-fetal outcomes in pregnant patients exposed to anti-TNF therapy. [36] Leukocyte trafficking agents selectively inhibit an adhesion protein (integrin α4β7) on a memory T cell subset, preventing the T cells' binding to gut mucosal cells. This action mechanism produces gut-specific anti-inflammatory effects. Vedolizumab is the agent in this class used in Crohn disease. This drug was found to be more effective than placebo in inducing and maintaining remission in Crohn disease, with or without immunomodulators. [37] However, the time to clinical effect may be slower (around 10 weeks) than anti-TNF agents, particularly in patients previously on anti-TNF therapy. [38] Gut-specific actions limit vedolizumab's toxicity. Thus, this drug's side effect profile is relatively favorable. IL-12/23 agents (ustekinumab, risankizumab) are efficacious in patients who failed prior corticosteroid, immunomodulator, or anti-TNF treatment. [39] [40] [41] Ustekinumab is a nonselective IL-12/23 inhibitor, while risankizumab is a selective IL-23 inhibitor. The side effect profile of these agents is relatively favorable according to safety surveillance data in psoriasis patients. [42] JAK inhibitors target Janus kinases that contribute to abnormal immune responses. Upadacitinib is the first FDA-approved oral selective JAK inhibitor therapy for Crohn disease. This medication is efficacious in patients who previously failed conventional and biologic Crohn disease therapies. [43] The onset of clinical effects is relatively rapid at 2 weeks. However, this drug increases Herpes Zoster risk. Thus, the shingles vaccine is recommended before treatment initiation. Use of upadacitinib during pregnancy is not advised due to findings of teratogenicity in animal studies. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical advancements for agents used in other autoimmune diseases, such as psoriatic arthritis (PA), atopic dermatitis (AD), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), giant cell arteritis (GCA), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), Crohn disease (CD) and ulcerative colitis (UC), have shown promising results. [3] Upadacitinib marks a significant milestone as the first oral medication approved by the FDA to treat moderate to severe Crohn disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Generalized pustular psoriasis [3] [1] Erythrodermic psoriasis [3] [1] Psoriatic arthritis [6] [7] [8] Moderate-to-severe scalp psoriasis [9] Alopecia areata and alopecia universalis [10] Nail psoriasis [11] [12] Oral psoriasis [13] Plaque psoriasis involving the palms and soles [12] Systemic lupus erythematosus [7] [14] Discoid lupus erythematosus [15] Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus [5] Lupus nephritis Inflammatory bowel disease (ie, Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis) [7] [16] Atopic dermatitis [16] Erosive oral lichen planus [17] Rheumatoid arthritis [16] [18] Dermatomyositis [18] Spondyloarthropathies (eg, ankylosing spondylitis) [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic Response Modifiers (BRMs) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: TNF inhibitors, e.g., infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab, certolizumab, and golimumab has been FDA approved for use in RA, ulcerative colitis (UC), Crohn disease (CD), AS, JIA, plaque psoriasis and, psoriatic arthritis. Adalimumab also has approval for hidradenitis suppurativa and uveitis. Non-FDA-approved indications include pyoderma gangrenosum, pustular psoriasis, and GVHD(etanercept). Interleukin-1 inhibitor, e.g., anakinra, is FDA approved for RA and neonatal-onset multisystem inflammatory disease. IL-2 inhibitor, e.g., aldesleukin, is used in metastatic renal cell carcinoma and melanoma. Tocilizumab is an interleukin-6 inhibitor used in giant cell arteritis, JIA, and RA. Secukinumab that targets interleukin-17 is FDA approved for psoriasis (plaque and arthritis), and AS. Ustekinumab blocks interleukin 12/23 and has been FDA-approved for plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and CD. Abatacept (T-cell co-stimulation blocker) has approval for use in RA, JIA, and Psoriatic arthritis. Rituximab is FDA approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, non-Hodgkins lymphoma, RA, granulomatous polyangiitis, and pemphigus vulgaris. Off label uses include Burkitt lymphoma, CNS lymphoma, GVHD, lupus nephritis, and Hodgkin lymphoma. Tofacitinib is Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors indicated for psoriatic arthritis, RA, and UC. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Sulfasalazine -- Indications -- Ulcerative colitis
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfasalazine was initially used for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, but further study discovered that it has some role in treating patients with inflammatory bowel disease. Sulfasalazine is FDA-approved for ulcerative colitis but not FDA-approved for Crohn disease. Two randomized controlled trials have shown that sulfasalazine was more effective than a placebo for the induction and maintenance of remission in ulcerative colitis. The current ulcerative colitis guideline suggests using sulfasalazine in patients in remission who are already on sulfasalazine or patients with dominant arthritic symptoms. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The American Academy of Dermatology (AAD) endorses using upadacitinib for adults with moderate to severe atopic dermatitis (AD). Upadacitinib is FDA-approved for patients with AD who have failed other systemic therapies, including biologics, or when the use of those therapies is inadvisable. [4] For patients with ulcerative colitis (UC) who experience recurrent pouchitis after ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA), the American Gastroenterological Association (AGA) suggests advanced immunosuppressive therapies like upadacitinib. Upadacitinib can be considered for chronic antibiotic-dependent pouchitis. Endoscopic evaluation is advised to confirm inflammation and rule out other causes. For patients with ulcerative colitis (UC) who have undergone IPAA and develop symptoms due to cuffitis, the AGA suggests using topical therapies approved for UC treatment, such as topical mesalamine and topical corticosteroids. Upadacitinib or other immunosuppressive therapies may be considered for refractory cases. [5] According to the American College of Rheumatology guidelines, targeted synthetic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (tsDMARDs), including JAK inhibitors like tofacitinib, baricitinib, and upadacitinib, are recommended as part of a treat-to-target (TTT) approach. This approach is strongly recommended over standard care for patients without prior treatment with biologic DMARDs (bDMARDs) or tsDMARDs. This recommendation extends to optimizing methotrexate dosage and subsequently adding DMARDs as needed. Although the evidence is of low certainty, this recommendation is maintained due to the recognized importance of systematic monitoring and treatment adjustment to minimize inflammation, thereby preventing joint damage and other long-term complications such as cardiovascular disease and osteoporosis. In DMARD-naive patients with moderate-to-high disease activity, methotrexate monotherapy is recommended. [6] The indication algorithm for rheumatoid arthritis is provided in Image. Upadacitinib for Rheumatoid Arthritis Algorithm. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity offers an in-depth examination of upadacitinib, a second-generation selective Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor targeting the JAK1 enzyme, approved by the FDA for treating various chronic inflammatory conditions such as moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis, atopic dermatitis, ankylosing spondylitis, Crohn disease, and ulcerative colitis. The session will cover the specific mechanism by which upadacitinib inhibits key intracellular cytoplasmic enzymes (Janus kinases) involved in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, disrupting the JAK-STAT signaling pathway and thereby reducing inflammatory responses.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Stepwise Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: A stepwise approach that is used for IBD management is outlined below.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Administration -- Ulcerative Colitis
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended dose of tofacitinib in the ulcerative colitis treatment is 10 mg twice daily for a duration of eight weeks initially, then decreased to 5 mg twice a day.

retrieved_docs.title: Brodalumab -- Contraindications -- Inflammatory Bowel Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Brodalumab is contraindicated in patients with Crohn disease. Researchers terminated a randomized, placebo-controlled trial examining the safety and efficacy of brodalumab in patients with Crohn disease due to disproportionate Crohn disease progression in those patients treated with brodalumab: 31% experienced worsening of disease versus 9% in the placebo group. Additionally, though patients with Crohn disease met the exclusion criteria from the psoriasis clinical trials, during one phase 3 study, one patient developed new-onset Crohn disease. If a patient develops Crohn disease while taking brodalumab, they should discontinue therapy. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Administration -- Ulcerative Colitis
retrieved_docs.content: Important to note that tofacitinib 10 mg twice daily is only FDA approved for ulcerative colitis treatment. Tofacitinib treatment should be discontinued if an adequate therapeutic response is not achieved by 16 weeks of therapy at the 10 mg twice a day dosage. Subjects with moderate to severe renal impairment or moderate hepatic impairment are recommended half of the daily dosage of subjects receiving treatment within the normal renal and hepatic function range.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tofacitinib is FDA approved for the treatment of moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA). It is a second-generation selective Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor targeting the JAK1 enzyme. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent to interprofessional team members in the management of patients with moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular course juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA) that is unresponsive to first-line therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Stepwise Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: The immune-modifying agents (eg anti-TNF agents) are the step three drugs. These are used when the patient does not respond to corticosteroids, steroids are required for prolonged periods, or the steroids cannot be tapered down without recurrence of symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2269
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the sulfasalazine compound, indicating that these 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives constitute the active moiety released from sulfasalazine after colonic bacterial metabolism. Sulfasalazine, a combination of sulfapyridine and 5‑aminosalicylic acid, is employed in the treatment of ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, and its therapeutic effect relies on the release of aminosalicylic acid and related agents within the intestine. Recognizing aminosalicylic acid and its analogues as integral components of sulfasalazine helps clinicians understand the drug’s mechanism of action and potential side‑effect profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2847
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is identified as a member of the corticosteroid group that is formulated for topical use in the treatment of hemorrhoids and anal fissures. In addition to its established role in managing these anorectal conditions, triamcinolone may also be employed as a therapeutic option for multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). This dual applicability highlights the drug’s versatility within the corticosteroid class, linking its anti‑inflammatory properties to both local anorectal therapy and potential systemic use in demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1729
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 544
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the mesalamin group and function as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excluding corticosteroids, are also categorized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Additionally, corticosteroids acting locally are recognized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Together, these drug classes—mesalamin derivatives, antiallergic non‑corticosteroid agents, and locally acting corticosteroids—share the therapeutic role of reducing inflammation within the gastrointestinal tract, highlighting the diverse pharmacologic approaches available for managing intestinal inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Fluokortolon and desoximetason are both classified as potent corticosteroids and are included in the category “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations.” As members of this group, they share the pharmacologic characteristics of strong glucocorticoid agents, providing powerful anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and often being incorporated into various combination drug formulations. Recognizing their membership in this potent corticosteroid class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens and anticipating the potency‑related safety considerations associated with high‑strength glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1660
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excl. corticosteroids, and corticosteroids acting locally are both classified as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. These two categories represent distinct subgroups within the broader class of intestinal antiinflammatory agents, highlighting the diversity of therapeutic options used to manage intestinal inflammation. Recognizing that antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs (excluding corticosteroids) and locally acting corticosteroids each belong to this class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory treatments based on the specific pharmacologic properties and intended use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 101
retrieved_docs.content: intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excl. corticosteroids” and “Corticosteroids acting locally” are both subclasses of intestinal antiinflammatory agents, reflecting overlapping mechanisms of local anti‑inflammatory activity in the gastrointestinal tract. Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor that functions as an immunological agent, immunomodulator and immunosuppressive drug, underscoring its role in modulating immune‑cell interactions. Finally, the Arabic‑named therapeutic approach الصَّدَفِيَّة is noted as a potential treatment modality associated with betamethason‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1659
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of both the mesalamin (mesalamine) group and the sulfasalazine group, reflecting their role as 5‑aminosalicylic acid derivatives that are incorporated into these related therapeutic classes used in the management of inflammatory bowel disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 450
retrieved_docs.content: distinguishing products by their potency and intended route of administration. This integrated classification supports precise prescribing, formulation selection, and patient‑centered management of inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1471
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) may be treated with triamcinolon, a drug that belongs to the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids, represented by both triamcinolon and rimexolone, share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are employed in the management of various inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. Recognizing that triamcinolon and rimexolone are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group informs clinicians in selecting appropriate steroid therapy for conditions such as multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as “triamcinolon,” is classified within the group of moderately potent corticosteroids that are used in various combination formulations (“Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”). As a member of this corticosteroid class, triamcinolone possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that make it a therapeutic option for managing multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By recognizing triamcinolone’s place among moderately potent corticosteroids, clinicians can better understand its mechanism of action, appropriate dosing, and potential side‑effect profile when it is employed to reduce inflammation and disease activity in patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2151
retrieved_docs.content: Antiastmatika, a class of anti‑asthmatic agents, includes betamethason as a specific member of this therapeutic group. Although primarily recognized for its anti‑asthmatic properties, betamethason is also employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), illustrating its dual utility in both respiratory and dermatologic treatment. This overlap underscores the importance for clinicians to consider betamethason’s broader therapeutic spectrum when addressing patients who may have concurrent asthma and psoriasis, thereby supporting more comprehensive and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1883
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfasalazine and alvimopan anhydrous are both classified as gastrointestinální látky, indicating that they belong to the therapeutic class of gastrointestinal agents used to treat disorders of the digestive system. The therapeutic class gastrointestinální látky therefore encompasses these two drugs. In addition, sapropterin is also linked to the therapeutic class gastrointestinální látky, suggesting that it is considered part of the same broader drug category, either through a shared mechanism of action or potential off‑label gastrointestinal applications. Together, these relationships define sulfasalazine, alvimopan anhydrous, and sapropterin as members of the gastrointestinal therapeutic class, highlighting their relevance in managing gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.
